why: The label is a raw query-string token, not a description of the business scenario under test; a reader cannot infer the situation being exercised.
"redirect" names the mechanism, not the precondition or scenario, so the reader does not know when or why a redirect occurs.
suggested_fix: context "when an authenticated customer visits the root path" do

offending_code:
let!(:order) { create(:order, customer: customer) }
let!(:customer) { create(:customer) }
why: :order is declared with let! before :customer is defined, so when RSpec eagerly evaluates :order the :customer memoization does not yet exist, causing a NameError or incorrect fixture.
suggested_fix:
let!(:customer) { create(:customer) }
let!(:order)    { create(:order, customer: customer) }

why: Asserting on the URL couples the test to routing details; if the route path changes while the page content stays identical, the test breaks falsely.
Forcing a controller ivar bypasses the real code path that produces @order_count; bugs in that path are invisible to this test.
suggested_fix:
Remove instance_variable_set and create the orders through the factory so the controller computes the count naturally:

4.times { create(:order, customer: customer) }  # one already exists from let!(:order)
visit dashboard_path
expect(page).to have_content("5 orders")

offending_code:
controller.instance_variable_set(:@order_count, 5)
visit dashboard_path
expect(page).to have_content("5 orders", wait: 3)
why: The test injects a value directly into the controller's internal state instead of deriving it from real data, so it does not exercise the actual order-counting logic.
Stubbing the internal authorization predicate couples both tests to the private method name; if the authorization model is restructured the stub silently passes without testing real access control.
suggested_fix:
Remove the stub and set up the user in a state where can_view? returns true through real data or a public permission API:

customer.user.grant_permission(:dashboard)  # or the equivalent for this app's auth system

why: The magic wait: 3 has no justification; if the page renders synchronously the wait is unnecessary noise.
suggested_fix:
Drop the timeout if the page is synchronous:

expect(page).to have_content("5 orders")

If the page genuinely has an async operation, document why:

expect(page).to have_content("5 orders", wait: 3)  # order-count widget polls every 2 s
